title: Call to ban lead generators for super
full_text:
Pushy boiler room superannuation lead generators would be banned under a sweeping set of plans the Super Members Council has mooted.
But the peak body, which represents some of the biggest industry super players in Australia’s retirement sector, has warned changes should stop short of removing some of their favourite recruiting tools.
In its submissions to Treasury, the SMC said the laws governing superannuation in Australia had critical gaps, which enabled alleged frauds like First Guardian and the Shield Master Fund to flourish. They were enabled by a network of financial advisers and call centres pushing savers out of the highly regulated sector into less regulated spaces.
SMC chief executive Misha Schubert said safety gaps in the superannuation system were being exploited.
“We urgently need to level the playing field and lift protections and accountability in the parts of the system where they are weakest, bringing them up to the same high level as the strong safeguards that apply in mainstream super funds so that every Australian can have confidence their super is safe, protected and working hard in their best interests,” she said.
The SMC has also called for the Albanese government to finally progress reforms to financial advice, warning Australia’s current laws enabled poor operators to flourish.
The council said the government should complete its reforms to financial advice, which have only been half implemented despite plans announced in December 2023 to develop the remainder of the legislation. “A central driver of harm is the lack of access to safe, affordable financial advice through overdue (Delivering Better Financial Outcomes) reforms. In that vacuum, other actors have stepped in – often with poor or conflicted incentives,” the SMC said.
The SMC said while governments had attempted to stamp out the heavy sales tactics of the past with anti-hawking laws, the same issue had resurfaced in a different form.
The council said the government should not create a licensing regime for lead generators, warning it would not stop harm.
Two lead generators feeding clients into Shield and First Guardian were licensed. The council said current regulations were “being gamed”, warning lead generators were breaking sales process into multiple steps to be seen as legally compliant.
This can see ads placed on social media and data harvested, before retirees are targeted by a sales team who then handball them to a financial adviser who pays the lead generator a cut of the fees which are extracted from a customers’ super savings.
The SMC said all lead generation should be banned, but called for a carve out for “safe, legitimate education and communication activities”.
“Incremental changes – such as adding a bit more disclosure or tighter licencing rules – simply will not be enough to stop this terrible harm and would risk just shifting dangerous pressure sales into new forms,” Ms Schubert said. “The most effective fix is to ban this conduct outright – to stop the harm before it can happen.” Convicted remuneration and outsized fee payments, tied to rewarding advisers who convince clients to move into risky products should also be eliminated, the SMC said. It also targets “trustee for hire” arrangements, warning outsourcing eroded the oversight of retirement savings.
